narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 88% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitments like planning to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family, with a typical background as a white female applicant. However, your academic record stands out as the main concern: you're in the bottom decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, far below the average student's mid-range performance. This low third-year decile ranking was the single biggest factor influencing the model's decision, strongly signaling a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers. Next, your LSAT score of 35, which is below the typical 36.7 average, further pushed the prediction toward failure by suggesting challenges with the rigorous reasoning needed for law success. Your first-year decile being similarly in the lowest group reinforced this, making early academic struggles a key red flag. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, lower than the average 3.21, added to the concern, as it hints at consistent hurdles in handling demanding coursework. Your family income level played a moderate role, not helping offset these academics since it's around average, while your race and gender were less influential as they align closely with the majority of students who pass. Overall, these academic metrics, especially the year-three decile, outweighed your more typical demographic traits when stacked against successful applicants. To improve your odds next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost your score through targeted practice, build a stronger GPA narrative with post-undergrad courses or explanations, and demonstrate improved study habits via test prep or extracurriculars that show law readiness. Many students in similar spots have turned things around by prioritizing bar exam-specific preparation early, like joining study groups or using proven resources. With dedication to shoring up these academic areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal dreams. Hang in there—your full-time commitment is a great foundation.
